narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 34, and plans to attend full-time. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 99.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This high failure probability stems from several key aspects of your academic record that stood out to the model as risk factors when compared to typical applicants. Most importantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of around 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance lagged behind most peers. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also much lower than the typical 5.7, reinforced this concern as the next biggest influence, suggesting early struggles that didn't improve. Your family income bracket, while upper-middle and solid, still nudged the odds slightly toward failure compared to higher brackets common among successful bar takers. Even your LSAT score of 34, though respectable, fell short of the average 36.7 and contributed to the risk, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 versus the usual 3.21—both pulling in the same unfavorable direction, albeit less forcefully. Your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) and had minimal impact. Overall, your profile ranks lower academically than typical admitted students who pass the bar, especially in school deciles where averages hover in the mid-5s. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background, but a data-driven flag on these metrics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or boosting your GPA through post-bac courses to realign with stronger performers, then reapply to schools with more flexible criteria. You're capable of turning this around—many do with targeted improvements. Keep pushing; your story isn't over.
